title: Decision Tree Explorer for CLV Segment Analysis
text:
This prototype focuses on the Decision Tree Analysis module of our final visual analytics project.

The aim of this module is to help users identify which customer characteristics are most strongly associated with different customer lifetime value (CLV) segments.

A decision tree approach is selected because it is highly interpretable and can translate predictive patterns into simple if-then business rules.

title: Decision Tree Explorer for CLV Segment Analysis
text:
This prototype focuses on the Decision Tree Analysis module of our final visual analytics project.

The aim of this module is to help users identify which customer characteristics are most strongly associated with different customer lifetime value (CLV) segments.

A decision tree approach is selected because it is highly interpretable and can translate predictive patterns into simple if-then business rules.

title: Decision Tree Explorer for CLV Segment Analysis
text:
2.Data Source

2.1 Data Source and Preparation:
This prototype is based on two datasets:

customer_data.csv
transactions_data.csv

The current prototype primarily uses **`customer_data.csv`** as the modeling table because it already contains customer-level demographic, product, behavioural, satisfaction, and value-related variables. The transaction-level dataset, `transactions_data.csv`, is reserved for future extension, where additional aggregated transaction features may be engineered and merged into the modeling dataset.


2.2 Analytical target
The selected target variable is: clv_segment
This variable is suitable for a decision tree prototype because:

it is categorical,
it has direct business meaning,
it supports interpretable multi-class classification,
it aligns well with the group project’s customer value analysis theme.

The key analytical question is:
Which customer characteristics are most strongly associated with different CLV segments?


2.3 Variable selection
To reduce unnecessary complexity in the first prototype, predictors are selected from several meaningful groups:

demographic variables,
product holding variables,
app usage and engagement variables,
transaction behaviour variables,
satisfaction and support-related variables.

Example predictors include:

age
gender
location
income_bracket
occupation
education_level
household_size
savings_account
credit_card
investment_account
active_products
app_logins_frequency
feature_usage_diversity
bill_payment_user
auto_savings_enabled
tx_count
avg_tx_value
total_tx_volume
transaction_frequency
preferred_transaction_type
customer_tenure
satisfaction_score
nps_score
support_tickets_count
app_store_rating
feedback_sentiment



2.4 Excluded variables and leakage prevention
Some variables are excluded to avoid data leakage or analytical redundancy. These include:

customer_id
customer_lifetime_value
first_tx
last_tx
first_transaction_date
last_transaction_date

The main reason for excluding these fields is that they either function as identifiers or are too directly related to the target definition. Including such variables would artificially inflate model performance and reduce the interpretability value of the prototype.


2.5 Data cleaning and preprocessing
The following preprocessing steps are applied:

remove identifier and leakage-prone columns,
convert the target variable into a factor,
convert character variables into factors,
handle missing values,
prepare the dataset for train-test split.

For missing value handling, a simple but robust approach is adopted:

numeric variables: imputed using the median,
categorical variables: assigned an "Unknown" category.

This strategy is sufficient for the prototype stage because the main objective is to validate analytical feasibility and support UI design, rather than maximize model performance.

title: Decision Tree Explorer for CLV Segment Analysis
text:
3. Analytical Design

3.1 Why decision tree?
This prototype adopts a classification tree model for the clv_segment target. The main reason is that decision trees provide a strong balance between analytical usefulness and interpretability.
A decision tree is especially suitable in this project context for the following reasons:

it produces human-readable rules,
it helps users understand which variables drive customer classification,
it can be displayed visually in an intuitive way,
it supports interactive parameter tuning in a Shiny environment.

Unlike more complex ensemble models, the tree structure itself is part of the analytical output. This makes it appropriate for visual analytics, where users are expected not only to obtain results, but also to interpret the model logic.


3.2 Design emphasis: interpretability over accuracy
The goal of this module is not simply to maximize predictive accuracy. Instead, the design emphasizes:

transparency,
visual interpretability,
user interaction,
business explanation.

For this reason, the prototype allows the user to adjust parameters such as:

maximum tree depth,
minimum split size,
complexity parameter,
predictor group selection.

By changing these parameters, users can compare a simpler tree against a more complex tree and evaluate the trade-off between readability and classification performance.


3.3 User-oriented analytical outputs
The prototype is designed to generate several types of outputs:

Tree visualization
Model performance summary
Rules table
Business insights section

This design reflects the needs of both technical and non-technical users. A technical user may want to inspect model behaviour, while a business stakeholder may be more interested in the high-level decision rules.

title: Decision Tree Explorer for CLV Segment Analysis
text:
4. Prototype Implementation
This section presents the technical implementation of the decision tree prototype.
The workflow includes:

loading required R packages
loading the datasets
performing data preprocessing
preparing the modeling dataset
training a classification tree
evaluating model performance
visualizing the tree structure
extracting terminal node summaries for interpretation

The goal of this implementation is not to build the most accurate predictive model, but rather to validate the feasibility of an interpretable decision tree module that can later be integrated into the final Shiny visual analytics application.


4.1 Loading Required R Packages
The following R packages are used in this prototype:

tidyverse – data manipulation and cleaning

forcats – factor handling

rpart – decision tree modeling

rpart.plot – tree visualization

caret – model evaluation and confusion matrix

DT – interactive table output for later Shiny integration


pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,
  forcats,
  rpart,
  rpart.plot,
  caret,
  DT
)



4.2 Loading the Dataset
Two datasets are provided in the project:

customer_data.csv – customer-level features
transactions_data.csv – transaction-level records

For the current prototype, the primary modeling dataset is customer_data.csv, because it already contains aggregated behavioral and demographic variables.

The customer_data table contains customer-level attributes including:

demographic information
product holdings
transaction behavior summaries
satisfaction indicators
customer value metrics
CLV segment label



4.3 Data Preprocessing
To prepare the dataset for modeling, several preprocessing steps are performed.
These steps include:

removing identifier and leakage-prone variables
converting categorical variables to factors
handling missing values
preparing the final modeling table

Variables that may cause data leakage are removed before modeling. These include identifiers and variables that directly encode customer lifetime value.

customer_model <- customer_data %>%
  
  select(
    -customer_id,
    -customer_lifetime_value,
    -first_tx,
    -last_tx,
    -first_transaction_date,
    -last_transaction_date
  ) %>%
  
  mutate(
    clv_segment = as.factor(clv_segment)
  ) %>%
  
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) %>%
  
  mutate(
    location = forcats::fct_lump_n(location, n = 10),
    occupation = forcats::fct_lump_n(occupation, n = 10)
  )

Handling Missing Values
Missing values are handled using simple imputation strategies suitable for a prototype stage.

Numeric variables → imputed with median
Factor variables → assigned “Unknown” category


for (col in names(customer_model)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(customer_model[[col]])) {
    
    customer_model[[col]][is.na(customer_model[[col]])] <-
      median(customer_model[[col]], na.rm = TRUE)
    
  } else if (is.factor(customer_model[[col]])) {
    
    customer_model[[col]] <-
      fct_na_value_to_level(customer_model[[col]], level = "Unknown")
  }
}

After preprocessing, the dataset is ready for modeling.

To construct the classification tree, a set of meaningful predictors is selected from demographic, product, behavioral, and satisfaction-related features.

  select(all_of(c("clv_segment", predictors))) %>%

4.5 Train-Test Split
The dataset is split into training and testing subsets to evaluate classification performance.
A 70% training / 30% testing split is used.

set.seed(123)

train_index <- createDataPartition(
  model_df$clv_segment,
  p = 0.7,
  list = FALSE
)

train_data <- model_df[train_index, ]
test_data  <- model_df[-train_index, ]

dim(train_data)

[1] 34107    27

dim(test_data)

[1] 14616    27




4.6 Training the Decision Tree
A classification tree is trained using the rpart package.
Key parameters used in this prototype include:

maxdepth = 4
minsplit = 30
cp = 0.01

These values help maintain a moderately interpretable tree structure.

tree_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("clv_segment ~", paste(predictors, collapse = " + "))
)

tree_model <- rpart(
  tree_formula,
  data = train_data,
  method = "class",
  control = rpart.control(
    maxdepth = 4,
    minsplit = 30,
    cp = 0.01
  )
)

The confusion matrix provides several useful metrics, including:

overall accuracy
sensitivity and specificity
class-level performance

These metrics help assess whether the decision tree provides reasonable classification results.


4.8 Decision Tree Visualization
One of the most important outputs of this module is the tree visualization, which allows users to understand how the classification is performed.

rpart.plot(
  tree_model,
  type = 2,
  extra = 104,
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  box.palette = "GnBu"
)








The tree diagram shows:

which variables are used for splitting,
how customers are divided into segments,
which terminal nodes correspond to specific CLV groups.

This visual structure makes the model highly interpretable.


4.9 Extracting Terminal Node Rules
To support rule interpretation, information from the terminal nodes of the tree can be extracted.

leaf_nodes <- tibble(
  node = rownames(tree_model$frame),
  split_variable = tree_model$frame$var,
  sample_size = tree_model$frame$n,
  predicted_class = tree_model$frame$yval
) %>%
  filter(split_variable == "<leaf>")

Each terminal node corresponds to a set of implicit classification rules derived from the path of splits leading to that node.
These rules can later be translated into human-readable statements such as:

In the final Shiny application, these rules will be presented in a more interactive and interpretable format.


4.10 Summary
This section demonstrates a full implementation pipeline for the decision tree prototype:

loading datasets
preprocessing data
selecting predictors
training a classification tree
evaluating model performance
visualizing the decision tree
extracting terminal node information

The results confirm that a decision tree model can be successfully constructed using the available customer-level dataset. The tree structure also provides interpretable patterns that can be integrated into the final visual analytics dashboard.

title: Project Proposal
text:
The rapid growth of financial technology (fintech) services has transformed how individuals interact with financial institutions. Digital banking platforms, mobile payment systems, and online financial services generate large volumes of behavioural data that can provide valuable insights into customer usage patterns and financial behaviour.
Understanding these patterns is critical for fintech companies, as it enables them to better segment customers, personalize services, and improve customer value management strategies. However, many fintech platforms still struggle to effectively translate raw behavioural data into interpretable insights that can guide business decisions.
This project aims to explore customer behaviour in a fintech environment using a comprehensive dataset containing both customer-level attributes and detailed transaction records. Through visual analytics and interpretable machine learning techniques, the project seeks to uncover meaningful behavioural patterns and identify key drivers of customer value segmentation.

title: Project Proposal
text:
The rapid growth of financial technology (fintech) services has transformed how individuals interact with financial institutions. Digital banking platforms, mobile payment systems, and online financial services generate large volumes of behavioural data that can provide valuable insights into customer usage patterns and financial behaviour.
Understanding these patterns is critical for fintech companies, as it enables them to better segment customers, personalize services, and improve customer value management strategies. However, many fintech platforms still struggle to effectively translate raw behavioural data into interpretable insights that can guide business decisions.
This project aims to explore customer behaviour in a fintech environment using a comprehensive dataset containing both customer-level attributes and detailed transaction records. Through visual analytics and interpretable machine learning techniques, the project seeks to uncover meaningful behavioural patterns and identify key drivers of customer value segmentation.

title: Project Proposal
text:
2. Dataset Description
The analysis uses a fintech customer behaviour dataset composed of two related tables:

Customer-level dataset (customer_data.csv)
Transaction-level dataset (transactions_data.csv)

The customer-level dataset includes demographic attributes, product ownership information, satisfaction indicators, service interactions, and customer lifetime value (CLV) segmentation variables.
The transaction-level dataset records detailed financial activities such as transaction amounts, transaction types, and usage frequency over time.
To support behavioural analysis, transaction-level data will be aggregated into customer-level behavioural metrics (e.g., transaction frequency, average spending, and transaction diversity). These engineered features will then be merged with the customer-level dataset to create an integrated analytical dataset.

title: Project Proposal
text:
3. Project Objectives
The primary objectives of this project are:

To explore key patterns in fintech customer behaviour through exploratory data analysis (EDA).
To identify meaningful customer segments using clustering techniques.
To examine the key factors associated with customer lifetime value (CLV) segments using decision tree modelling.
To develop an interactive analytical prototype that allows users to explore segmentation patterns and predictive rules.

Through these objectives, the project aims to demonstrate how visual analytics and interpretable machine learning methods can support data-driven decision making in fintech environments.

title: Project Proposal
text:
4. Analytical Framework
The analytical workflow of this project consists of three main components:

4.1 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
The EDA stage focuses on understanding the structure and characteristics of the dataset. This includes:

data cleaning and standardization,
transaction aggregation into behavioural metrics,
examination of variable distributions,
correlation analysis between financial and behavioural variables.

The goal of this stage is to uncover initial insights into customer behaviour and prepare the dataset for further analysis.



4.2 Customer Segmentation using Clustering
Clustering techniques will be applied to group customers with similar behavioural and demographic characteristics.
By combining demographic variables, product ownership indicators, and transaction-derived behavioural features, clustering can reveal natural groupings within the customer base.
These clusters provide insight into different types of fintech users, such as highly engaged digital users, moderate users, or low-engagement customers.



4.3 CLV Segment Analysis using Decision Trees
To further understand the drivers of customer value, a decision tree model will be developed to analyse the relationship between customer characteristics and CLV segments.
Decision trees are selected because they provide:

high interpretability,
clear if–then decision rules,
intuitive visualization of predictive structures.

The model will help answer the key analytical question:
Which customer characteristics are most strongly associated with different CLV segments?
The resulting decision rules can provide interpretable insights into how demographic attributes, financial behaviour, and service interactions influence customer value classification.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5. Expected Outcomes
This project is expected to produce several analytical outputs:

visual exploration of fintech customer behaviour,
behavioural customer segmentation using clustering,
interpretable predictive rules explaining CLV segmentation,
an interactive Shiny-based analytical prototype.

Together, these outputs aim to demonstrate how visual analytics techniques can transform fintech behavioural data into meaningful insights that support strategic decision-making.

title: Project Proposal
text:
6. Prototype Design
The final deliverable of this project will be an interactive analytical prototype implemented using R Shiny.
The prototype will allow users to:

explore customer segmentation results,
visualize behavioural clusters,
inspect decision tree structures,
interpret key decision rules for CLV classification.

This interactive interface is designed to support exploratory analysis and help users better understand the relationship between customer characteristics and fintech usage behaviour.

title: Understanding Fintech Users in Colombia: An Interactive Visual Analytics Study Using the COFINFAD Dataset
text:
Understanding Fintech Users in Colombia: An Interactive Visual Analytics Study Using the COFINFAD Dataset

Project Workflow: EDA → Clustering → Decision Tree → Insights
This project explores fintech usage patterns in Colombia using the COFINFAD (Colombian Fintech Financial Analytics Dataset). The objective is to understand customer behaviour, identify meaningful user segments, and uncover key factors associated with fintech adoption.
The analysis begins with Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) to examine the structure of the dataset and identify important patterns and relationships among financial and demographic variables. Clustering techniques are then applied to discover natural groupings of users based on their financial behaviour and service usage.
To further interpret these patterns, a decision tree model is developed to identify the most important factors that influence fintech user segmentation. The model provides interpretable rules that help explain how different customer characteristics contribute to fintech usage patterns.
Together, these analytical steps provide a comprehensive view of fintech behaviour in Colombian Area. The results are presented through interactive visualisations and an analytical prototype designed to support data-driven insights and decision-making.

The goal of the module is to segment customers into behaviorally distinct groups by combining customer-level attributes with transaction-derived features. The analysis uses two related datasets: a customer-level table containing demographic, product, satisfaction, and service variables, and a transaction-level table containing detailed transaction records. By integrating these two sources, the clustering workflow is able to move beyond simple demographic profiling and capture richer patterns of customer behavior.

  group_by(customer_id, month) %>%
  summarise(
    monthly_n = n(),
    monthly_amount = sum(amount, na.rm = TRUE),

3.Analysis

3.1 Transformation and Standarzation

p1 <- ggplot(customer2, aes(x = tx_n)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of transaction count")

  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +

p3 <- ggplot(customer2, aes(x = tx_avg_amount)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of average transaction amount")

p1 + p2 + p3








The distributions of transaction count, total transaction amount, and average transaction amount are all highly right-skewed. Therefore, we use log transformation and standardization.

cluster_vars_refined <- c(
  "age",
  "active_products",
  "app_logins_frequency",
  "feature_usage_diversity",
  "credit_utilization_ratio",

  "failed_transactions",
  "satisfaction_score",
  "support_tickets_count",
  "weekend_transaction_ratio",
  "customer_tenure",
  "tx_n",
  "tx_total_amount",
  "tx_avg_amount",
  "share_Deposit",
  "share_Payment",
  "share_Transfer",
  "share_Withdrawal"
)

cluster_df <- customer2 %>%
  select(customer_id, all_of(cluster_vars_refined))

cluster_df2 <- cluster_df %>%
  mutate(
    credit_utilization_ratio = ifelse(
      is.na(credit_utilization_ratio),
      median(credit_utilization_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
      credit_utilization_ratio
    ),
    international_transactions = log1p(international_transactions),
    failed_transactions = log1p(failed_transactions),
    support_tickets_count = log1p(support_tickets_count),
    tx_n = log1p(tx_n),
    tx_total_amount = log1p(tx_total_amount),
    tx_avg_amount = log1p(tx_avg_amount)
  )
p1_log <- ggplot(cluster_df2, aes(x = tx_n)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  labs(title = "Transaction count after log1p transformation")

p2_log <- ggplot(cluster_df2, aes(x = tx_total_amount)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  labs(title = "Total transaction amount after log1p transformation")

p3_log <- ggplot(cluster_df2, aes(x = tx_avg_amount)) +

p1_log + p2_log + p3_log

The clustering workflow followed four preprocessing steps before model estimation. First, clustering variables were selected based on behavioral relevance. Second, missing values in credit_utilization_ratio were imputed using the median. Third, highly right-skewed variables were transformed using log1p() to reduce the influence of extreme values. Fourth, all retained variables were standardized so that no single measure would dominate the Euclidean distance calculation. A correlation review was then conducted on the processed variables to identify and remove highly redundant features.

K=3 solution was ultimately selected. Compared with the k=4 solution, the three-cluster structure provides clearer behavioral differentiation and more interpretable segment profiles. In particular, thek=3 model separates customers into lower-engagement lower-value users, frequent small-transaction users, and high-value power users. By contrast, the additional cluster in the k=4 solution mainly creates finer splits without introducing a substantively new behavioral segment.


3.3.2 PCA plot

pca_res <- prcomp(cluster_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

pca_plot_df <- as_tibble(pca_res$x[, 1:2]) %>%
  bind_cols(cluster = customer_clustered_3$cluster)

ggplot(pca_plot_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Customer clusters projected onto PCA space",
    x = "Principal Component 1",
    y = "Principal Component 2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()











3.4 Cluster Interpretation

3.4.1 Heatmap

profile_heat_3 <- customer_clustered_3 %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    feature_usage_diversity = mean(feature_usage_diversity, na.rm = TRUE),
    satisfaction_score = mean(satisfaction_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    support_tickets_count = mean(support_tickets_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    customer_tenure = mean(customer_tenure, na.rm = TRUE),
    churn_probability = mean(churn_probability, na.rm = TRUE),
    customer_lifetime_value = mean(customer_lifetime_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_n = mean(tx_n, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_total_amount = mean(tx_total_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    tx_avg_amount = mean(tx_avg_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cluster, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  mutate(z = as.numeric(scale(value))) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(profile_heat_3, aes(x = variable, y = cluster, fill = z)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "steelblue", mid = "white", high = "firebrick", midpoint = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Standardized cluster profile heatmap (k = 3)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Cluster",
    fill = "Relative level"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))








Cluster 3 stands out as the most valuable segment, with the highest relative levels of transaction frequency, total transaction amount, average transaction amount, customer lifetime value, and satisfaction score. This segment is therefore interpreted as a high-value power-user group. Cluster 2 shows a different pattern: it has the highest churn probability, the highest feature usage diversity, and relatively elevated support-ticket activity, yet its monetary indicators remain comparatively weak. This suggests a segment of active but at-risk customers. By contrast, Cluster 1 exhibits consistently lower levels across most engagement and value-related indicators, indicating a lower-engagement basic-user segment.


3.4.2 Boxplot

p1 <- ggplot(customer_clustered_3, aes(x = cluster, y = tx_n, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Transaction count by cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p2 <- ggplot(customer_clustered_3, aes(x = cluster, y = tx_total_amount, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Total transaction amount by cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p3 <- ggplot(customer_clustered_3, aes(x = cluster, y = customer_lifetime_value, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Customer lifetime value by cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p1 + p2 + p3








The boxplots reinforce the behavioral interpretation of the selected clusters. Cluster 3 clearly dominates in transaction count, total transaction amount, and customer lifetime value, indicating that it represents the most commercially important customer group. Clusters 1 and 2 remain substantially lower on these value-related measures, although Cluster 2 appears to interact more actively with platform features and support services. Together, these visual comparisons confirm that the selected k=3solution separates customers into meaningfully different value and engagement profiles.

title: Proposal_Clustering
text:
4 Conclusion
This clustering module demonstrates that meaningful customer segmentation can be achieved by combining customer-level attributes with transaction-derived behavioral features. The final k=3 solution identifies three interpretable segments: Low-Engagement Basic Users, At-Risk Active Users, and High-Value Power Users. These segments differ not only in transaction behavior and monetary value, but also in feature usage diversity, support interaction, satisfaction, and churn risk. As a prototype module for the final Shiny application, this clustering workflow provides a strong foundation for interactive exploration of customer heterogeneity and for linking behavioral segments with downstream predictive analysis.

title: Proposal_Clustering
text:
5.UI Design
The proposed Shiny UI for the clustering module should support both overview and drill-down analysis. On the input side, users should be able to select the profiling metric of interest, switch between visualization modes, and optionally filter by customer characteristics such as segment or acquisition channel. On the output side, the module should provide:
(1) a cluster size summary chart,
(2) a PCA projection plot for structural overview,
(3) a standardized profile heatmap for segment comparison,
(4) boxplots for selected key indicators such as transaction count, transaction amount, and customer lifetime value, and
(5) a summary table describing the characteristics of each segment.
This design would allow end users to understand not only how large each customer segment is, but also how the segments differ in engagement, value, and risk.

title: Proposal_Storyboard
text:
This prototype is designed for multiple types of users:

instructors evaluating the technical and visual design quality
classmates and project teammates
general business users
non-technical stakeholders who need interpretable insights

Therefore, the UI is designed to be simple, structured, and exploration-oriented.





The proposed Shiny layout consists of two main areas.


The sidebar contains user controls such as:

predictor group selection

maximum tree depth

minimum split size

complexity parameter

train-test ratio

optional data filters (e.g., gender or income group)




The main area contains tabbed outputs, including:

Tree Plot
Performance
Rules
Data Preview
Business Insights

This layout supports an intuitive workflow: users first control the model settings and then inspect the analytical outputs from multiple perspectives.





The intended user interaction sequence is:

The user selects a predictor group.

The user adjusts tree complexity settings.

The system fits the decision tree model.

The tree plot and performance metrics are updated.

The user reviews the resulting rules and business insights.

This sequence is designed to encourage exploration rather than passive viewing.






The dashboard opens with a title, a short module description, and a default tree plot.
The sidebar displays all control settings.



The user changes tree depth and complexity settings.
The tree is regenerated, allowing comparison between simpler and more detailed decision structures.



The user switches to the Performance tab to review confusion matrix results and determine whether the current tree is sufficiently effective.



The user opens the Rules tab and examines terminal node summaries or extracted if-then logic for business interpretation.



The user reads a short textual summary that translates model patterns into practical managerial insights.





This UI design is suitable because it balances:

analytical depth
visual clarity
user control
interpretability

Instead of overwhelming users with too many outputs at once, the tab-based layout separates analytical tasks into manageable views.
This makes the module more usable and better aligned with the purpose of visual analytics.
